insights.insight: Ad tech charges $5–$150 CPA for probabilistic attribution; this protocol settles at roughly $0.10 per verified receipt — a 50–200x cost delta that isn't incremental improvement, it's a different category of signal entirely.

Cookies are dead, fingerprinting is degrading, and first-party data is blind outside a single ecosystem — none of the incumbent stacks produce identity-free, cryptographically verified purchase signals across vendors, which is the specific gap this dataset fills.

17 active CBSAs with a demonstrably rural spine and no metro cluster anchor — ad tech data is structurally metro-biased, so this dataset offers a geographic distribution that legacy targeting infrastructure cannot replicate.

Amazon sees Amazon purchases, Kroger sees Kroger purchases; at 4,038 verified receipts across King Soopers, Walmart, Kroger, Amazon, Target, Costco and others, this dataset aggregates cross-retailer spend without requiring any retailer's cooperation.

Five SaaS vendor requests (Notion, Render, Vercel, DigitalOcean, Hetzner) are open but unactivated — if they convert, the dataset becomes the first verified signal combining physical and digital commerce in a single queryable layer, a combination that does not currently exist in the market.

One active brand predicate querying verified purchase history confirms real demand, but the predicate lifecycle — whether it expired, completed, or was cancelled — is not yet visible, making this a signal to watch rather than a confirmed pattern.
